No Poverty.reason: The course explicitly covers the World Bank and IMF, key development finance institutions whose mandates include poverty reduction and resource mobilization for developing countries (Targets 1.a, 1.b).
evidence: How to Revitalize the World Bank, the IMF, and the Development Finance System

Zero Hunger.reason: The course syllabus and readings do not contain any explicit or strong implicit references to hunger, food security, sustainable agriculture, or malnutrition.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: The course syllabus and readings lack explicit or strong implicit connections to health-related topics, disease prevention, or well-being initiatives.

Quality Education.reason: While the course provides education, it does not explicitly or implicitly cover topics related to SDG 4 targets, such as access to quality education, literacy, or educational infrastructure.

Gender Equality.reason: The course syllabus and readings do not contain explicit or strong implicit references to gender equality, women's empowerment, or related issues.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: The course syllabus and readings do not contain any explicit or strong implicit references to clean water, sanitation, or water resource management.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: The course syllabus and readings do not contain any explicit or strong implicit references to affordable and clean energy or related topics.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course explicitly covers the World Trade Organization and globalization, which are central to international trade policies and economic growth, aligning with targets like 8.a.
evidence: Why the World Still Needs Trade: The Case for Reimagining—Not Abandoning—Globalization

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: The course's discussion of the 'Development Finance System' (World Bank, IMF) provides a minimal inferred link to supporting infrastructure development in developing countries (Target 9.a).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The course explicitly discusses reforming global financial institutions (World Bank, IMF) and the role of G20/BRICS, directly addressing enhanced representation for developing countries (Target 10.6) and reducing global inequalities.
evidence: How to Revitalize the World Bank, the IMF, and the Development Finance System

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: The course syllabus and readings do not contain any explicit or strong implicit references to sustainable cities, communities, or urban development.

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: The course syllabus and readings do not contain any explicit or strong implicit references to responsible consumption, production patterns, or waste management.

Climate Action.reason: The course syllabus and readings do not contain any explicit or strong implicit references to climate action, climate change, or related environmental policies.

Life Below Water.reason: The course syllabus and readings do not contain any explicit or strong implicit references to marine ecosystems, ocean health, or life below water.

Life on Land.reason: The course syllabus and readings do not contain any explicit or strong implicit references to terrestrial ecosystems, biodiversity, or life on land.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The course is fundamentally about international organizations, global governance, security, and international law (UN, NATO), directly addressing the establishment of effective, accountable institutions and the rule of law (Targets 16.3, 16.6, 16.8).
evidence: United Nations – Security in the Charter

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course's core objective is 'intergovernmental cooperation' and the study of various international organizations (UN, WTO, G20, EU, etc.), which are direct examples of global partnerships for sustainable development (Targets 17.10, 17.13, 17.16).
